title: A Non-flag Arithmetic Regularity Lemma and Counting Lemma
speaker: Daniel Altman
abstract: We will discuss a version of the Green--Tao arithmetic regularity lemma and counting lemma which works in the generality of all linear forms. In this talk we will focus on the qualitative and algebraic aspects of the result.

proof_stages.label: Reduction target: linear-pattern averages
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with weighted counts of four-term progressions, generalizes them to arbitrary systems of linear forms, recalls generalized von Neumann control by Gowers norms, and explains how the arithmetic regularity lemma reduces such averages to nilsequence averages.

proof_stages.label: Nilsequence orbit-analysis problem
proof_stages.summary: Nilsequences are introduced via torus phases and nilmanifolds, and the Liebman/Green--Tao equidistribution-factorization principle is used to formulate the local problem of describing the orbit of a polynomial nilsequence after evaluation at a system of linear forms.

proof_stages.label: Flag systems and the Green--Tao Liebman group
proof_stages.summary: For flagged systems, where the coordinatewise power spans satisfy the required nesting, Green--Tao's filtration-based theorem describes the limiting nilmanifold as a Liebman group, provided the polynomial sequence is suitably irrational with respect to that filtration.

proof_stages.label: Failure of coefficient data in the non-flag case
proof_stages.summary: A non-flag system and a Heisenberg calculation show that knowing where each coefficient of a polynomial sequence equidistributes in the Lie group is not enough to determine the distribution after composition with the linear forms.

proof_stages.label: Passage to Lie-algebra rational structures
proof_stages.summary: The speaker replaces lattice-level nilmanifold data by commensurability classes and rational structures in the Lie algebra, allowing equidistribution to be phrased in linear-algebraic terms.

proof_stages.label: Linear irrationality and the non-flag equidistribution theorem
proof_stages.summary: A new notion of linear irrationality for Lie-algebra coefficients is defined, and the main qualitative theorem states that a linearly irrational polynomial sequence composed with any linear pattern equidistributes in the connected subalgebra generated by the corresponding coefficient subspaces tensored with the power spans of the pattern.

proof_stages.label: Factorization for arbitrary polynomial nilsequences
proof_stages.summary: To make the equidistribution theorem usable in the regularity strategy, an arbitrary polynomial sequence is factorized into a periodic/rational part and a linearly irrational part, so averages may be analyzed on connected components after preprocessing.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and scope of the non-flag replacement
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion explains that this repairs the non-flag gap needed for the Gowers--Wolf true-complexity problem, while noting that applications may require extra algebraic work and that extensions to polynomial patterns are possible only under restrictions preventing degree interference.

examples.name: Diagonal and mod-three torus orbits
examples.note: Sequences in a torus product show why one must allow equidistribution on subgroups and on cosets after passing to subprogressions, motivating the factorization viewpoint.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through the R^2/Z^2 orbit, identifying diagonal equidistribution and then mod-three foliations into cosets

passes.reason: proper diagonal and mod-three coset orbits collapse to subtori/cosets and show the finite-or-full-torus dichotomy fails
The orbit is confined to a diagonal subtorus or finitely many cosets, refuting the naive finite-or-full-torus dichotomy.

The diagonal and mod-three torus orbits show the finite-or-full-torus dichotomy fails, since the orbit is trapped in a proper subtorus or its cosets.

examples.name: R mod Z versus rationally rescaled lattices
examples.note: The observation that equidistribution modulo Z is equivalent to equidistribution modulo 2Z or any rational multiple of Z grounds the abstract passage from lattices to rational structures.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the commensurable-lattice/rational-structure discussion to the concrete abelian case R/Z versus rational rescalings such as R/2Z

passes.reason: commensurable rational rescalings of the one-dimensional lattice are a standard illustration of the same rational structure
R mod Z and rational rescalings are the rank-one abelian base case for the commensurable-lattice/rational-structure discussion.

examples.name: Product of two tori and the filtration nesting problem
examples.note: A question about the source of the flag hypothesis is answered by an abelian two-torus example where linear and quadratic coefficients live in different factors, forcing Green--Tao's nested filtration to be too large in the non-flag case.
examples.engagement_reason: he works through the product-of-two-tori setup to show how linear and quadratic components force a nested filtration that becomes too large

passes.reason: the split product-of-tori sequence forces filtration nesting to add spurious directions, showing the flag-based description can fail

The split product-torus sequence separates linear and quadratic factors, showing nested filtrations can overpredict in non-flag systems.
the split product-torus example shows that filtration nesting can force too large a Liebman group for nonflag systems
The split product-torus example shows that forcing a nested Green--Tao filtration can give the wrong orbit description in the non-flag setting.

examples.name: Polynomial patterns with degree interference
examples.note: In the final Q&A, polynomial-form patterns are contrasted according to whether squaring one form creates terms such as y^2 that overlap with terms from another form of different degree, clarifying the limits of a line-by-line extension.
examples.engagement_reason: he tests polynomial patterns by expanding degrees, explaining when terms such as y^2 from a squared middle form interfere with another coordinate

passes.reason: polynomial patterns with overlapping degree terms obstruct the line-by-line extension of the linear-form argument
The polynomial pattern has degree collisions, such as a y^2 term appearing from different sources, which blocks the line-by-line extension.

Overlapping monomials from distinct degrees create a dependent polynomial pattern blocking the naive polynomial-form extension.
overlapping monomials from different degrees create an algebraic dependence that obstructs a direct polynomial extension
